name: serverless
context: Create Vercel Serverless Functions — API routes, handlers, and runtimes (Node.js, Python, Go, Ruby)
info: Vercel Serverless Functions run backend code on-demand without managing servers. Functions are defined as files in the `api/` directory (standalone) or as Next.js API routes in `pages/api/` or `app/api/`. Each function receives an HTTP request and returns a response. Supported runtimes include Node.js (default), Python, Go, and Ruby. Functions scale to zero when idle and auto-scale under load. Cold starts vary by runtime and bundle size. Environment variables are available at runtime via `process.env` (Node.js) or `os.environ` (Python). Functions have a default timeout of 10 seconds (Hobby) or 60 seconds (Pro/Enterprise). See [[vercel/env-vars]] for environment configuration.
triggers: add an api route on vercel
write a vercel function in python

sections.title: Creating functions
sections.body: Place files in `api/` for standalone functions or use Next.js API routes (`pages/api/` or `app/api/`). Each file becomes an HTTP endpoint. The file path maps to the URL path: `api/users/[id].js` handles `/api/users/:id`. Export a default handler function (Node.js) or a `handler` class (Python).

sections.title: Supported runtimes
sections.body: Node.js (`.js`, `.ts`) is the default and fastest runtime. Python (`.py`), Go (`.go`), and Ruby (`.rb`) are also supported. Specify the runtime version in `vercel.json` with the `functions` config. TypeScript is compiled automatically — no build step required for `.ts` files in the `api/` directory.

sections.title: Request and response handling
sections.body: Node.js functions use Express-compatible `(req, res)` objects. Access query parameters via `req.query`, parsed body via `req.body`, and headers via `req.headers`. Next.js App Router uses Web standard `Request` and `NextResponse`. Set CORS headers manually or use the `vercel.json` headers config. See [[vercel/env-vars]] for accessing secrets at runtime.

sections.title: Environment variables in functions
sections.body: Access environment variables via `process.env` (Node.js), `os.environ` (Python), or `os.Getenv()` (Go). Variables are injected at runtime based on the deployment scope (development, preview, production). Configure via [[vercel/env-vars]]. Encrypted secrets are decrypted automatically at runtime.

sections.title: Cold starts and performance
sections.body: Functions scale to zero after inactivity. Cold start latency ranges from 100ms (small Node.js) to 2s+ (large Python bundles). Reduce cold starts by minimizing dependencies, using tree-shaking, and lazy-loading heavy modules. For sub-50ms latency requirements, use [[vercel/edge]] functions which run on V8 isolates with near-zero cold starts.
